title: Milk Money

jokes.text: They call this a candidates' breakfast?!
jokes.setup: Moira arrives at a small-town political event expecting something befitting her status
jokes.punchline: Her outrage at the ordinariness of the event — delivered as if she were at the Oscars — is the joke
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira character work — the line is economical and perfectly in voice, but it's a familiar beat of wealthy-person contempt for ordinary things. Strong opener, not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What is this? A... a municipal gathering? In a church basement? With folding chairs and store-bought refreshments?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've attended galas on yachts with less tragic energy than this.

jokes.text: Oh, bless you, dear, but a name tag does not go with this ensemble.
jokes.setup: Twyla cheerfully offers Moira a name tag as standard event protocol
jokes.punchline: Moira refuses on fashion grounds, treating a paper sticker as an aesthetic incompatibility
jokes.explanation: A reliable Moira beat — her priorities are always fashion over function. The 'bless you, dear' condescension layered onto the refusal is nicely in character. Not original but well-delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, what do you want me to do about it? I'm not going to wear cargo shorts.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's exactly the problem.

jokes.text: It's just so people know who you are. Not that people won't know, when they see you in that outfit. It's very shiny.
jokes.setup: Twyla explains the practical purpose of the name tag
jokes.punchline: She inadvertently (or perhaps knowingly) implies Moira's outfit is so outrageous she's recognizable anyway — then lands on 'It's very shiny' with devastating simplicity
jokes.explanation: Twyla's deadpan is perfectly deployed here. The pivot from practical explanation to backhanded compliment to 'It's very shiny' has a nice three-beat structure. The understatement of 'shiny' as a descriptor for what is presumably a spectacular outfit is funny in its smallness.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Here's your name tag.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A name tag? Twyla, this is a fashion emergency. Do you have any idea what this will do to my silhouette? It's going to create an unfortunate visual interruption right at the bust line.

jokes.text: You're too kind.
jokes.setup: Twyla has just described Moira's look as 'very shiny' — a strange non-compliment
jokes.punchline: Moira receives 'very shiny' as a genuine compliment, which reveals either her vanity or her obliviousness
jokes.explanation: A two-word reaction that lands because of what it reveals about Moira's character. Her self-regard is so total that any description of her appearance registers as flattery. Economy is the primary virtue here.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, she was wearing a large feathered headdress, and a sequined bodysuit that was mostly sheer, with like, seven-inch platform heels, and she had glitter all over her face.

jokes.text: Well, I'm glad you talked me out of the Mohawk.
jokes.setup: Moira has appeared in an already outrageous outfit
jokes.punchline: The revelation that a Mohawk was also seriously considered — and rejected — implies the outfit IS the restrained option
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely strong joke. The logic is perfect: the audience is already processing Moira's wild outfit as extreme, and then the joke reframes it as the COMPROMISE position. The Mohawk implies an even wilder version exists in Moira's imagination. Character-specific (only Moira would consider a Mohawk for a small-town political breakfast), structurally tight, and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I considered a Mohawk for the political event, but I thought it might be too subtle.

jokes.text: I think these people deserve a little glamour in their political campaign.
jokes.setup: The breakfast is a modest small-town event; Moira has arrived overdressed
jokes.punchline: She frames her self-expression as an act of generosity toward the townspeople — she's doing THEM a favor
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira self-justification structure — her vanity is always dressed up as altruism. The framing is recognizable but executed with conviction.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I thought, 'What if I brought a little glamour to politics?' And then I remembered those parties in the Hamptons, you know, the... social gatherings. Very inclusive. Everyone was welcome. Very open-minded. And I thought, 'That's the template.'

jokes.text: I was always impressed by those masquerade key parties at the Sarkosy's.
jokes.setup: Moira is discussing political campaigning and wanting to make an impression
jokes.punchline: She cites 'masquerade key parties' (i.e., swinger parties) as her reference point for glamorous political events, either oblivious to or perfectly aware of the implication
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. 'Key parties' is a euphemism for swinger sex parties, and Moira deploys it with complete casualness as a reference point for a political event. The joke works on multiple levels: her past life's decadence, her obliviousness (or knowingness), and the juxtaposition with a small-town breakfast. The specificity of 'Sarkosy's' (naming the hosts as if a real social landmark) elevates it further. One of the episode's best.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, well, well, look who's here.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Moira, you're looking very... Elvis today.

jokes.text: Hey Johnny, good morning to you. And Elvis! I'm so glad you could make it.
jokes.setup: Roland greets Johnny normally, then appears to address a third person — 'Elvis'
jokes.punchline: It becomes clear he's referring to Moira's outfit making her look like Elvis
jokes.explanation: A mild misdirection gag. The fake-out of greeting someone who isn't there is a classic structure, and comparing Moira to Elvis is a reasonable punchline given the outfit descriptions. Not original, but Roland's oblivious delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, Moira, you're a lot like Elvis.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, the whole... the whole Vegas thing, the jumpsuits, the, uh... the way people just kind of stare at you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Not in a bad way! I mean, Elvis was... he was very loved. Very respected. Although, you know, towards the end, he did let himself go a little bit, and—no, no, I'm not saying you're letting yourself go. You look great. You always look great. I just meant the legacy thing. You know, how some people remember you one way and then...

jokes.text: Well, because you look like Elvis in that outfit there, you know, and... but the early Elvis, not the...
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to explain why he called Moira 'Elvis'
jokes.punchline: The clarification 'but the early Elvis, not the...' trails off — he can't complete the sentence but has still invoked fat, jumpsuit Vegas Elvis as the implied alternative
jokes.explanation: Roland digging deeper into his faux pas by specifying 'early Elvis' — then being unable to finish the sentence — is a nice extension. The audience fills in 'not the fat, dying one' which is funnier than if he'd said it. Good instinct to leave it unfinished.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, she's getting attention, isn't she? I call that a hit.

jokes.text: It's raw milk we're talking here, it's all natural, unpasteurized, straight from the cow's breast, to your mouth.
jokes.setup: Bob is explaining why the milk cost seven dollars
jokes.punchline: 'Straight from the cow's breast, to your mouth' — the phrasing is weirdly intimate and slightly sensual for a milk transaction
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'cow's breast' instead of 'cow's udder' is a small but funny choice — it anthropomorphizes the cow and makes the milk-drinking sound intimate in a weird way. Bob's earnest delivery of this as a selling point is well-played.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: This raw milk comes from cows with the most beautiful breasts.

jokes.text: Yeah, okay, Bob, you know, I get it, but... we're not talking heroin here.
jokes.setup: Bob is treating the procurement of raw milk with extreme gravity and secrecy
jokes.punchline: Johnny deflates the drama by pointing out this is milk, not heroin — but the fact he has to say it at all is the joke
jokes.explanation: Johnny as the straight man deflating Bob's paranoia is a reliable structure, and the heroin comparison is an effective escalation of the absurdity. Economy is strong — five words do the work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Bob, do you understand the gravity of what you've done? Raw milk is incredibly dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: It's just milk from a local farm, Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: JUST milk? Bob, you're a criminal. A hardened criminal. I'm going to have to distance myself from you.

jokes.text: Maybe you're used to being on the wrong side of the law — but I for one am not going to prison. Gwen has already told me she won't wait for me.
jokes.setup: Johnny has minimized the milk situation; Bob pushes back
jokes.punchline: Bob has already had a conversation with his wife about whether she'd wait if he went to prison — for milk
jokes.explanation: The escalation is excellent: Bob has not only feared prison but has already negotiated his marital status in a prison scenario — all over raw milk. 'Gwen has already told me she won't wait for me' is a great line because it implies a real conversation happened. The specificity of Gwen's name adds to the lived-in comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: If I go to prison for milk, my girlfriend won't wait for me.

jokes.text: Right, okay Bob, I'll keep it on the down low.
jokes.setup: Bob has begged Johnny to be discreet about the illegal milk
jokes.punchline: Johnny using slang like 'on the down low' for raw milk is funny given the absurd gravity of the situation
jokes.explanation: A functional button to the milk scene but not particularly inventive. The juxtaposition of drug-deal slang with milk works as a mild callback to the heroin comparison, but the joke has been somewhat deflated by the better preceding lines.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm very discrete. I can keep a secret about milk.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You need some whole milk? I know a guy. He's got the good stuff, keeps it in the back. Very fresh. Moves it fast.

jokes.text: You know, if you expect me to drive you to work, it would be nice if you were ready on time. / I am ready! / I just have to get my jacket, change out of these shoes, and grab my bag.
jokes.setup: Stevie complains David isn't ready; David insists he is
jokes.punchline: He immediately lists three things he still needs to do before leaving
jokes.explanation: A classic self-contradiction gag, but David's specific list (jacket, shoes, bag — three whole things) after claiming readiness is well-executed. The list structure makes it better than a simple 'I am ready / no I'm not' exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not difficult, I'm just particular. There's a difference.

jokes.text: I am approachable... [beat] Is that a question? / Yes, the question is: I am approachable... [beat] That sounds more like a fact that you're sharing with me.
jokes.setup: Moira wants David's opinion on her approachability as a political candidate
jokes.punchline: Her 'question' is structured as a declarative statement, and David calls it out with perfect precision — 'that sounds more like a fact you're sharing with me'
jokes.explanation: Excellent comic structure. Moira's 'question' reveals she isn't actually asking — she's seeking confirmation of what she already believes. David's observation is linguistically precise and perfectly in character for him (sharp, slightly academic). The repeat of the question as a non-question is well-paced. Strong mother-son dynamic comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's not really a question, so I'm not sure how you expect me to answer it.

jokes.text: Yes! As a strong willed modern woman, with a high school degree, you know how hard we have to work to be taken seriously!
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to find common ground with Stevie as a woman in the workforce
jokes.punchline: She describes Stevie as having 'a high school degree' — condescending enough, but delivered as if it's a badge of honor they share
jokes.explanation: Moira's oblivious condescension is perfectly pitched — she's trying to bond with Stevie while inadvertently dismissing her. The 'high school degree' framing is a nice class-consciousness joke from this show's core DNA.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I'm just like you. I'm an average... Jane.

jokes.text: I actually went to college, but sure.
jokes.setup: Moira has just described Stevie as having 'a high school degree' as if it's their shared qualification
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat correction — 'I actually went to college, but sure' — is the perfect deadpan response, accepting the absurdity rather than fighting it
jokes.explanation: The 'but sure' is the key — Stevie corrects Moira but immediately surrenders to the chaos. This is a classic straight-man response in Schitt's Creek's style: not angry, just slightly deflated acceptance. The economy is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the masses are also following a guy on TikTok who eats cereal with orange juice, so.

jokes.text: I would vote for you based solely on the fact that you wore this outfit to the café for breakfast.
jokes.setup: Moira has been asking if she's approachable; Stevie is now being pressed for an opinion
jokes.punchline: Stevie's endorsement is entirely based on the absurdity of Moira's fashion choice, not any political quality
jokes.explanation: A nice Stevie line — she can't quite bring herself to give a real endorsement, so she redirects to the most honest thing she can say. The specificity ('to the café for breakfast' — not a gala, not a red carpet) makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I'm going to simplify this for you. When I was your age, I was already fluent in three languages. Well, two and a half if you count my brief Esperanto phase. But the point is, education is really about expanding one's vocabulary and cultural references.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Moira, I went to college.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, a community college, dear. That's very... resourceful of you.

jokes.text: You know, being approachable isn't that important anyway. The queen hasn't smiled since the 70's, and her birthdays are still very well attended.
jokes.setup: The conversation is about whether Moira needs to be more approachable as a candidate
jokes.punchline: David offers the Queen of England as a counterexample to approachability mattering, then undercuts it with 'her birthdays are still very well attended' as if that's the relevant metric
jokes.explanation: A strong David line. The Queen comparison is absurd but has a weird internal logic (both Moira and the Queen project imperious glamour). The punchline — 'her birthdays are still very well attended' — is a gloriously specific, strange metric for political success. The deadpan delivery implied in the writing is perfectly David.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Stevie, I went to a very prestigious boarding school in Switzerland. We had a curriculum that was quite rigorous. But I'm sure your high school experience was... quaint in its own way.

jokes.text: Exactly! Wait. What are you saying?
jokes.setup: David has compared Moira to the notoriously unapproachable Queen
jokes.punchline: Moira initially takes it as a compliment (of course), then has a delayed realization — the beat between 'Exactly!' and 'Wait' is the joke
jokes.explanation: The delayed take is a reliable structure, and it works beautifully here. Moira's instantaneous pride followed by the slow dawn of doubt is a perfect character beat. The 'Wait' is doing enormous comedic heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is that your girlfriend?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, that's my business partner.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, so she's a lesbian too?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, she's just my business partner.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's still very modern.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, thanks for the compliment... I think.

jokes.text: Oh my god! Ted? / Alexis! / You look good. Like, really good, like, super... sun kissed, and muscle-y.
jokes.setup: Alexis encounters her ex-fiancé Ted, whom she broke off their engagement, looking significantly better than when she last saw him
jokes.punchline: Her spiral into 'sun kissed, and muscle-y' reveals she is flustered and clearly affected — the comedy is her loss of composure
jokes.explanation: Alexis's verbal stumble into 'muscle-y' is a funny character moment. The ex looking unexpectedly good is a familiar comedic premise but Alexis's specific verbal deterioration makes it her own.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not unapproachable. I'm just... discerning about who I interact with. The Queen of England has the same demeanor, and people seem to respect her just fine.

jokes.text: 'Cause they actually gave me the Broken Hearts Special. Three extra nights at no extra charge, as long as I could prove that I didn't actually make it down the aisle.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions he went on the couples' vacation Alexis cancelled by breaking off their engagement
jokes.punchline: The resort had a specific 'Broken Hearts Special' package — implying this happens often enough to market — and Ted benefited from Alexis's cruelty
jokes.explanation: The 'Broken Hearts Special' is a wonderfully specific comic invention — the idea that getting dumped before your wedding gets you a deal is dark but optimistic. The condition 'as long as I could prove he didn't make it down the aisle' is an absurd bureaucratic detail that elevates the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, hi! I didn't know you'd be here.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was just... I had to pick up some files.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right. Of course. That makes total sense. You look... I mean, your hair looks... Did you do something different?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, it's the same.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, no, I knew that. I just meant... anyway. So how have you been? Because I've been great. Really great. Not thinking about you at all.

jokes.text: I got almost full price back for the engagement ring, and I've always wanted one of these [the motorcycle], and it cost less than the ring, so...
jokes.setup: Ted explains where he got the motorcycle
jokes.punchline: He financed his post-breakup dream purchase by returning the engagement ring he'd bought for Alexis — and the motorcycle cost less, implying a windfall
jokes.explanation: The economic chain from broken engagement → ring return → motorcycle is a nicely logical piece of dark comedy. Ted's complete lack of bitterness about this transaction is the key — he's genuinely thriving, which is worse for Alexis than anger would be.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So I booked us this amazing resort package. Turns out it was specifically for jilted grooms.

jokes.text: My day's been stacked, doing rounds to some farms, checking on the animals, and now some house calls. But I gotta say, it's a lot more fun now that I've got this puppy. Pun intended.
jokes.setup: Ted describes his busy day and mentions his motorcycle
jokes.punchline: He calls his motorcycle 'this puppy' — a generic slang term — and then flags 'pun intended' because he's a vet and 'puppy' has a literal meaning in his profession
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character integration. The 'pun intended' is funny because the pun only exists because Ted is a vet — it's not a pun for anyone else. It reveals his personality (earnest, slightly dorky, good-natured) while being genuinely clever. This is exactly what character-specific comedy should do.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I sold my engagement ring to buy this motorcycle. So really, Sheila breaking up with me was the best investment I ever made.

jokes.text: It's like if the Sons of Anarchy had a vet.
jokes.setup: Ted, a wholesome veterinarian, is now riding a motorcycle
jokes.punchline: Alexis compares him to Sons of Anarchy — a biker gang show — but the contradiction of 'had a vet' is the joke
jokes.explanation: A neat pop culture comparison that works because it's exactly right — Ted is all the rebellion aesthetics with none of the menace. 'Had a vet' is the essential detail that makes it funny rather than just flattering. Good Alexis line.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, you look so... cool.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah? I picked up a motorcycle.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's so sexy. I didn't know you had a dangerous side.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I'm taking it slow. I mostly just ride to the animal clinic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Yeah! Except they don't wear helmets on that show, Which I get, but, it's a little dangerous.
jokes.setup: Alexis has compared Ted to Sons of Anarchy
jokes.punchline: Ted's response engages sincerely with the safety critique of Sons of Anarchy — breaking the flattery to address helmet use
jokes.explanation: Ted's earnest safety concern about a fictional biker gang is perfect character comedy. His 'I get it, but it's a little dangerous' is exactly the kind of response only Ted would give — he's culturally aware but constitutionally unable to be reckless. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, bikers have a very high rate of head injuries, and most of them don't wear helmets, which is just... it's irresponsible. And the leather — sure, it looks cool, but it's not going to protect you from much. And don't even get me started on the motorcycles themselves. Do you know how many accidents I see? It's a lot.

jokes.text: David reveals he works at the store when Jocelyn asks if he's 'picking something up for himself'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks David if he's shopping at the store — an understandable assumption
jokes.punchline: David's flat admission 'No. No, I um... I work here now.' — followed by a beat — implies the embarrassment of someone accustomed to shopping at luxury retailers now working retail in a small town
jokes.explanation: The comic beat is in David's delivery — the pause and 'So...' at the end signal resignation. A reliable fish-out-of-water moment for the Rose family's decline. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, I bought it from a guy in a van in the Walmart parking lot.

jokes.text: I usually just come here when I need to kind of... freshen up my wardrobe.
jokes.setup: David now works at the store — a shop he used to be a customer at
jokes.punchline: He admits he still shops there for himself, as if that's the natural and expected relationship with one's employer
jokes.explanation: A mild character beat — David treating his workplace as a personal shopping resource is consistent characterization but not a strong punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I was just thinking about the time my dad tried to milk an elk. I'm not actually sure if that's something you can do with an elk. Can you milk an elk?

jokes.text: Twyla's black market milk story: 'I had a bad experience with black market milk... I bought it from a man who I later found out was a drifter... it might've been elk's milk.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Twyla about raw milk at the restaurant; she mentions a bad experience
jokes.punchline: The escalating revelation: she bought milk from a drifter, it made people sick, and it might have been elk's milk — each detail stranger than the last
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely excellent Twyla story. Each element escalates perfectly: the drifter (already wrong), people getting sick (worse), and 'I'm almost positive it wasn't cow's milk / it might've been elk's milk' (gloriously specific and absurd). Twyla's confessional delivery style — matter-of-fact about genuinely bizarre experiences — is one of the show's great comic assets. This is character comedy at its best.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm looking for milk. Fresh milk. The good stuff. You know what I'm talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You'd be a terrible drug dealer, Mr. Rose.

jokes.text: You would make a terrible drug dealer, Mr. Rose.
jokes.setup: Johnny has been loudly asking about raw milk in a public diner, treating it like a secret transaction
jokes.punchline: Twyla's flat assessment of his drug-dealer skills is simultaneously an insult, an absurd compliment, and a perfect character observation
jokes.explanation: An outstanding Twyla line. The economy is exceptional — eight words that characterize Johnny perfectly, comment on the absurdity of the situation, and reveal Twyla's odd frame of reference (she has a standard for drug dealers). Highly quotable and a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying you want to start small, maybe work your way up to a larger operation? Because honestly, Dad, the key is consistency. You need reliable suppliers, a solid customer base, and you definitely can't be doing business out of the same location every time. That's like, Drug Dealing 101.

jokes.text: You're thinking about becoming a drug dealer? I mean, I get it, it's fast money, and no one would ever suspect you.
jokes.setup: Alexis has overheard only part of the conversation about raw milk
jokes.punchline: She jumps to the drug dealer conclusion and then doubles down with 'no one would ever suspect you' — a backhanded compliment to her father's squareness
jokes.explanation: The misunderstanding gag is standard, but 'no one would ever suspect you' is a characteristically Alexis observation — she's simultaneously defending and insulting her father. The fact that she finds this plausible says something about both of them.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, if you're going to sell raw milk, you have to treat it like a major criminal enterprise. You need lookouts, code words, a whole distribution network.

jokes.text: I had a friend in Venice Beach who sold raw milk, and his entire compound was raided. I mean, he also sold drugs, but like...
jokes.setup: Alexis warns Johnny about the dangers of the raw milk business
jokes.punchline: 'He also sold drugs, but like...' — the 'but like' implies the drugs are an irrelevant detail; the raw milk was the real problem
jokes.explanation: The trailing 'but like...' is the key. Alexis treats the drug operation as a minor, irrelevant complication to the raw milk story — which is a perfect inversion of normal priorities. This is excellent characterization: Alexis's past experiences are so wild that actual drug dealing barely registers.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need to get in touch with Ted. Do you have his number?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, yeah, I think so. I dated him for like three weeks in 2015.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, can you just call him?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, I've dated a lot of people. I can't just call every guy I've ever been with.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why not? You seem to have kept in touch with most of them.

jokes.text: You know, your trail of discarded boyfriends, Alexis, is making it very difficult to get anything done in this world.
jokes.setup: Alexis says it would be awkward to call Ted for help with the milk situation
jokes.punchline: Johnny's exasperated complaint frames Alexis's romantic carnage as a logistical obstacle to his business plans
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully specific complaint that captures the show's family dynamic. 'Trail of discarded boyfriends' is a sharp phrase, and the framing of it as a professional inconvenience ('making it very difficult to get anything done') is a funny inversion of normal parental concern.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for helping me pick out this outfit, David. You have such great taste.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate that, but I should mention that I have no recollection of this interaction, and if anyone asks, I was not here.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: What? Why would you say that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you're wearing it to support your husband's business, and I have a reputation to protect.

jokes.text: Does this print come in a different colour? / Uh, theoretically.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is shopping and asks a reasonable question about a garment
jokes.punchline: David's 'theoretically' is a brilliant non-answer — it acknowledges the question without actually helping, implying the answer is yes in the abstract but no in practical terms
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best one-word punchlines. 'Theoretically' is perfectly David — evasive, slightly superior, and somehow both an answer and a non-answer. The economy is exceptional. It reveals David's personality (not actually trying to help) and his complicated position (he's supposed to be helping his mother's competitor). A small gem.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You brought things? Alexis, we discussed this. I asked you to bring necessities. Basic necessities.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I did bring necessities, Dad. These are things that I need.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You brought your winter shoe collection to a motel room in a town we may only be in for a few weeks.

jokes.text: For what? I didn't do anything.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn thanks David after he has clearly guided her entire shopping experience toward an outfit that will compete with Moira
jokes.punchline: David's denial is earnest — he's convinced himself he didn't help — which is funnier than if he were being obviously sarcastic
jokes.explanation: The payoff of David's extended self-deception. Five words that land perfectly because the audience knows exactly what he did and he insists he did nothing. The self-delusion is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, how much did you spend?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: On what?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Candles.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: For the store?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, for the store.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: How many candles are we talking?

jokes.text: Good afternoon, Jazzagals. I have a great warm-up exercise I'd like to share with you.
jokes.setup: Moira has arrived at the choir group and immediately takes charge
jokes.punchline: The 'warm-up exercise' she's about to introduce is clearly political campaigning — she's hijacking a choir rehearsal for her campaign
jokes.explanation: A setup for the broader scene rather than a punchline in itself. Moira's appropriation of the choir group for her own purposes is a reliable character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Three hundred dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, three thousand.

jokes.text: Well, of course, you all do know each other already, but I'd love for you to get to know me. Does anyone have any questions?
jokes.setup: Moira frames the 'get to know each other' exercise as being for her benefit
jokes.punchline: She explicitly states she wants them to get to know HER — the reversal of the normal social exercise — and then opens the floor for questions about herself
jokes.explanation: A nice inversion of the normal 'get to know each other' structure. Moira's narcissism is deployed with precision — she's completely sincere about wanting them to know her specifically.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, you destroyed the entire milk supply! Do you understand what you've done?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Daddy, I know, but honestly, I think you should be thanking me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thanking you? For what?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I could have just left it there, but instead I cleaned it up. You're welcome.

jokes.text: Moira produces a bottle of vodka to break the ice at the choir rehearsal
jokes.setup: The Jazzagals are awkwardly silent; Moira announces she has a 'break the ice' idea
jokes.punchline: Her solution is to pull out vodka — at a daytime choir rehearsal, before they've even done any singing
jokes.explanation: A visual reveal that tells us everything about Moira's social toolkit. The Jazzagals' immediate enthusiasm for the vodka ('Unless of course the drinks are free!') is a nice secondary beat.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm going in armed. Got my BB gun right here.

jokes.text: We don't usually drink until after rehearsal. / Oh. / Unless of course the drinks are free!
jokes.setup: A Jazzagal establishes a principled boundary about drinking before rehearsal
jokes.punchline: The principle evaporates immediately at the mention of free drinks
jokes.explanation: A classic comic pivot — establish a rule, immediately abandon it for a single exception. 'Unless of course the drinks are free!' has an enthusiastic specificity that elevates it above a generic 'well, okay then.' The pacing of the beat (Moira's 'Oh.' of disappointment before the reversal) works well.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've run dozens of checkpoints. Hundreds maybe. I know exactly what I'm doing.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Where's the... the checkpoint thing? And what am I looking for again?

jokes.text: What is this? / The twelve things of milk that you asked for. / The twelve 'things' of milk?
jokes.setup: Johnny asked for twelve pints; Alexis has apparently delivered something different
jokes.punchline: Alexis refers to large containers as 'things' — and Johnny's echo of 'things' is the first beat of dawning horror
jokes.explanation: A solid setup for the milk misunderstanding payoff. Alexis's 'things' is funny as a revelation of her disconnect from practical specifics, and Johnny's horrified echo is a good reaction beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I was in Johannesburg when I was 14, but that was before I got my braces off, so it was really more of a phase.

jokes.text: Ohhh! Okay, 'cause I was wondering why would Dad order $300.00 worth of milk?
jokes.setup: Johnny has revealed she ordered large containers instead of pints, buying $300+ of milk
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'Ohhh!' of comprehension is followed by the revelation she wondered about this the whole time but proceeded anyway
jokes.explanation: The delayed realization is funny, but what makes it excellent is the implication: Alexis thought '$300 of milk is weird' but placed the order anyway. Her 'I was wondering' admits she had the thought and ignored it. A perfect Alexis character beat — question present, follow-through absent.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: All three wives had the same name

jokes.text: Well, $394.40, so I guess kind of closer to $400.
jokes.setup: Johnny is horrified at $300 worth of milk
jokes.punchline: Alexis volunteers the more precise (and higher) figure, digging the hole deeper with specific accuracy about the wrong number
jokes.explanation: The escalation from '$300' to '$394.40, so closer to $400' is a reliable escalation structure. The specificity of $394.40 (she knows the exact amount) contrasted with her having thought 'things' was a unit of measurement is the underlying contradiction that makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. You had three wives, and they were all named Gayle?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: That's not a pattern, that's a fetish.

jokes.text: What am I gonna do with $400.00 worth of raw milk?! / Sell it! / To whom?! / To your customers! / I don't have any customers! / Well then, why would you order so much milk?! / I didn't! You did!
jokes.setup: Johnny and Alexis try to establish blame for the milk disaster
jokes.punchline: The circular argument where each question leads to a more incriminating answer, culminating in 'I didn't! You did!' — which is accurate but unhelpful
jokes.explanation: A rapid-fire escalation exchange that works because each answer is technically correct but makes things worse. The rhythm of the exchange is well-written — short lines, building speed, mutually assured confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, you look absolutely radiant. Is that from the store? Well, I mean, it certainly looks expensive.

jokes.text: Okay, I try and do something nice for somebody, you think I would at least deserve a 'thank you!' / Oh well, thank you, Alexis, thank you for ordering an insane amount of milk that I will never be able to sell! / You're welcome!
jokes.setup: Alexis has caused a milk disaster but pivots to being the aggrieved party
jokes.punchline: Johnny's sarcastic thank-you is devastating; Alexis's genuine 'You're welcome!' is the perfect button — she accepts it at face value
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure here is excellent. Alexis demanding thanks for causing a disaster is a strong character moment. Johnny's specificity in the sarcastic thank-you ('for ordering an insane amount of milk that I will never be able to sell') is well-written. But Alexis's genuine 'You're welcome!' is the killer punchline — she takes sarcasm at face value, which reveals her unselfawareness and ends the fight with perfect absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Officer, the name's Buck Henry.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Wait, that's my name too?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I wasn't going to give you a good one.

jokes.text: How many times do you want me to apologize?! / Well, try one, and let's see what happens.
jokes.setup: Johnny is exasperated at being asked to apologize for the milk fiasco
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'try one, and let's see what happens' reveals he hasn't actually apologized yet — just complained about being asked to
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical exchange. Johnny's complaint presupposes multiple apologies; Moira's response reveals he's done zero. 'Try one, and let's see what happens' is a near-perfect line — patient, sardonic, and devastating. The structure is impeccable: misdirection (he acts like he's over-apologized) → reveal (he hasn't apologized once). Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, no, you don't understand. My name isn't actually Roland Schitt.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's... it's Roland Schitt. That's the real one. The other one was fake.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? Why are you looking at me like that? I'm explaining myself!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The fake name was also Roland Schitt, but spelled different. With a 'y.' Rolynd.

jokes.text: We came to you because you have a truck. / And because you know if anybody can navigate the dark underworld of the raw milk game, it's me.
jokes.setup: Roland is positioning himself as the expert in dealing with the raw milk underground
jokes.punchline: Johnny deflates this immediately — they came for the truck. Roland responds by insisting on his raw milk expertise anyway.
jokes.explanation: 'The dark underworld of the raw milk game' is a glorious phrase — Roland's complete sincerity about raw milk as a criminal underworld is perfect character comedy. Johnny's pragmatic deflation adds the contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, it's Jennifer Rose. Jennifer Rose Goldstein. My parents are both doctors, and I grew up in Connecticut, and I went to Brown.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm actually a competitive figure skater, and I'm just taking a year off to find myself.

jokes.text: Just remember, if things go south, there's a loaded BB gun in the back.
jokes.setup: Roland is treating the milk delivery like a dangerous criminal operation
jokes.punchline: His backup plan is a BB gun — a child's toy — which he calls 'loaded' with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: The BB gun is a perfect detail — it fully commits to the absurdity of treating milk delivery as a dangerous mission while undercutting the menace completely. 'Loaded' is the key word; Roland's insistence on this descriptor is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: You're a dairy farmer in a Valentino suit?

jokes.text: Oh my God, it's the cops! / Don't stop! / No, it's nothing, it's probably an accident! / I'm gettin' out of here! / Roland, Roland! / Close the door! / You're acting like I've never done this before!
jokes.setup: The three are in the truck with illegal milk when they spot a police checkpoint
jokes.punchline: All three respond completely differently and simultaneously — Roland panics to flee, Johnny tries to reason, Alexis is worldly about checkpoints — creating overlapping chaos
jokes.explanation: The three-way character divergence in a crisis is effective — each character's response perfectly matches who they are. Alexis's 'You're acting like I've never done this before!' is the standout line, implying extensive checkpoint experience.

jokes.text: I've been through tons of these in Johannesburg, um, it's like a drive-thru, except everybody has a gun.
jokes.setup: Alexis is surprisingly calm about the police checkpoint
jokes.punchline: Her Johannesburg checkpoint experience — and describing it as 'like a drive-thru, except everybody has a gun' — reveals the terrifying normalcy of her past
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The comparison of a police checkpoint to a drive-thru 'except everybody has a gun' is both hilarious and slightly horrifying. It reveals Alexis's past as genuinely dangerous and explains her worldliness — she's been through actual armed checkpoints at age 14. The simile is perfectly constructed. Character integration is near-perfect: no other character on this show or any show would say this line.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: I'm a dairy farmer.

dialogue.speaker: Woman Officer
dialogue.line: You're wearing a cashmere sweater.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's a very nice dairy farm.

jokes.text: When were you in Johannesburg?! / I don't know, I remember I just got my braces off, so... / You were 14, in South Africa?!
jokes.setup: Alexis mentions her Johannesburg checkpoint experience
jokes.punchline: The revelation she was approximately 14 years old during this armed checkpoint experience — and that she can only date it by when she got her braces off
jokes.explanation: The braces detail is the key — it's a completely normal adolescent milestone used to anchor a deeply abnormal experience. Johnny's horrified '14, in South Africa?!' is the straight-man reaction that validates the joke. The escalation of Alexis's casual past continues building the character beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: Pull over, Jacob Jingleheimer Schmidt!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Jacob Jingleheimer Schmidt? That's not— wait, that's my fake name system. How do you know my fake name system?

jokes.text: Hey Johnny, Johnny, Johnny! If this turns into a fire fight, um... you're my human shield, and we'll use her as a bargaining chip.
jokes.setup: The group is approaching the police checkpoint with illegal milk
jokes.punchline: Roland has a pre-planned contingency: Johnny as human shield, Alexis as bargaining chip — volunteering them for harm with cheerful specificity
jokes.explanation: Roland's pre-planned hostage protocol for a milk delivery is wonderfully absurd. The specificity of the plan (one shield, one chip) implies forethought. Roland cheerfully sacrificing both Roses without hesitation is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, that ensemble looks like it was curated by a woman who shops exclusively at gas stations and believes that fabric softener is a personality trait.

jokes.text: So then my Uncle Ralph marries his third wife. / What do you think her name was? / I don't know. / Gayle! The man marries three Gayles!
jokes.setup: Moira is telling a story to the Jazzagals, building up to a reveal
jokes.punchline: Uncle Ralph's third wife is also named Gayle — he's married three women with the same name, apparently learning nothing
jokes.explanation: A classic setup/punchline with a good structure. The interactive element ('what do you think her name was?') builds audience anticipation. 'Like didn't learn anything from the first two!' is a strong button. Moira as a storyteller is an underused mode and it works well here. The Jazzagals laughing 'uproariously' confirms the impact.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A knife? David, a knife is what you use when someone has already stabbed you in the back. It's the tool of the betrayed, the weapon of the wounded. If you're asking me for a knife, then you've already lost the war.

jokes.text: So we've scrapped the whole approachable angle, have we?
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has arrived looking glamorous — clearly having received styling help — which directly competes with Moira's strategy
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately reads the competitive implication while pretending to comment on strategy
jokes.explanation: Moira immediately clocks the threat while maintaining social composure. The comment is framed as political strategy but is actually competitive fashion observation. A sharp line that works on two levels.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I was very convincing. I looked them right in the eye, didn't break character for a second.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I think I really threw them off with that bit where I said 'I definitely wasn't there' three times in a row.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And when I started sweating profusely and my eye twitched, I'm pretty sure that sealed it. Very authentic stuff.

jokes.text: It takes a real eye to find something of quality at a clothier in Elmdale.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says she found her outfit at a shop in Elmdale
jokes.punchline: Moira's compliment is a barely concealed insult — 'clothier in Elmdale' implies a low standard, so finding 'quality' there is a minor miracle she's generously acknowledging
jokes.explanation: Moira's backhanded compliments are a reliable source of comedy. 'Clothier in Elmdale' as a term for a small-town boutique has the right amount of condescension. Well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We need to search for those compromising photos before they surface.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose we could hire someone discrete. Though the last time we hired help, they turned out to be running a side business selling our vintage clothing to consignment shops.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not helpful, Mother.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm simply saying, David, that in this family we've learned that when you try to contain a problem, it usually results in someone's intimate apparel ending up on eBay with a detailed provenance.

jokes.text: Oh, just a little bit of luck, and the right... salesperson.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is responding to Moira's compliment about finding good clothes in Elmdale
jokes.punchline: Her reference to 'the right salesperson' — with a meaningful pause — is a direct but unspoken acknowledgment that David helped her, which Moira cannot yet fully parse
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'salesperson' is the joke — Jocelyn is clearly enjoying the tease. The audience is ahead of Moira, which creates dramatic irony. A good payoff beat for the subplot.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: We need to search for nude photos of myself on the internet.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick Brewer
dialogue.line: Why would there be nude photos of you on the internet?

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I don't know, Patrick. Why would there be a stranger in a storm cellar?

jokes.text: And here I thought you were against this type of campaigning. / Who says this has anything to do with the campaign?
jokes.setup: Moira brought vodka to campaign; Jocelyn got dressed up for the same reason — both deny their competitive motivations
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn mirrors Moira's earlier denial ('Who says this has anything to do with the campaign?') — a perfect callback that exposes both women's hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: An elegant callback structure where the same line is now used by both characters as mutual denial of obvious campaigning. The structural symmetry is satisfying and the hypocrisy of both women is exposed simultaneously. Well-crafted.

jokes.text: Just remember, no sudden movements, do not reach for the glove box, and no matter what happens do not tell them your real name.
jokes.setup: They are approaching a police checkpoint with illegal milk
jokes.punchline: Roland's instructions treat a routine checkpoint like a hostage negotiation — complete with a prohibition on giving real names, for a dairy delivery
jokes.explanation: Roland's escalating paranoia is perfectly characterized. The three-step instruction list (no sudden movements, no glove box, no real names) builds nicely, with the name prohibition being the absurdist escalation.

jokes.text: That's, uh, Jennifer. And then he's, um... Jacob. And then I'm also Jacob.
jokes.setup: The officer asks who's in the vehicle; Roland told them not to use their real names
jokes.punchline: Alexis assigns herself and Johnny different names, then gives herself and Johnny both the name Jacob — two Jacobs, different genders
jokes.explanation: The payoff of Roland's 'no real names' instruction. Alexis improvises names under pressure and somehow ends up with two Jacobs — herself (a woman) and Johnny — which makes no sense and is perfectly Alexis. The incremental naming structure (Jennifer... Jacob... also Jacob) is well-paced.

jokes.text: Well, you can look at our drivers' licences, if you don't believe me! / Oh, I don't think that'll be necessary, Jacob.
jokes.setup: Alexis has given fake names; the officer is skeptical
jokes.punchline: Alexis offers to show licenses — which would immediately disprove the names — and the officer declines, calling her 'Jacob' (the fake name)
jokes.explanation: Alexis offering licenses to verify names that are on the licenses (which would disprove her) is a nice bit of panic-logic. The officer's dry 'Jacob' acknowledgment is a good straight-man beat.

jokes.text: Jennifer? Actually, my birth name is Angelica.
jokes.setup: The officer turns to 'Jennifer' (Alexis's fake name); Alexis has apparently decided Jennifer isn't good enough
jokes.punchline: She immediately abandons her fake name and adopts a different one — 'actually, my birth name is Angelica' — as if Jennifer were a nickname she was correcting
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. Alexis abandons her cover story mid-interrogation and spontaneously creates a second fake name, framing Jennifer as a nickname for Angelica. The confidence with which she says 'my birth name is Angelica' — as if correcting a minor formality — is perfectly Alexis. This is the scene's best individual line.

jokes.text: Johnny's panicked over-denial about hunting: 'Hunting? No, no. We don't uh, hunt. I mean, there's n-no hunting going on here. No illegal... yeah, you won't find any illegal game here, that's for sure.'
jokes.setup: The officer asks if they've been hunting — a completely unrelated question
jokes.punchline: Johnny's over-denial of hunting — specifically including 'no illegal' — when they're actually carrying illegal milk is a perfect portrait of a bad liar
jokes.explanation: The classic 'too much denial' comic structure. Johnny's spiral from 'no hunting' to 'no illegal' to 'that's for sure' — volunteering the word 'illegal' when it wasn't asked — is a funny character beat for someone who was supposed to be cool about this.

jokes.text: Hard work, hard work, 'cause you get up, you know, so early, but uh... but we love it. / We do. / You farm in that suit?
jokes.setup: Johnny and Alexis are claiming to be dairy farmers; the officer is skeptical
jokes.punchline: The officer's single question — 'You farm in that suit?' — demolishes the cover story with perfect economy
jokes.explanation: Five words that end the conversation. The officer's deadpan is the perfect straight-man response to Johnny's elaborate false premise. 'You farm in that suit?' captures the entire absurdity of a city person pretending to be a farmer. The economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: When the weather's good.
jokes.setup: The officer has asked 'you farm in that suit?' — an unanswerable question
jokes.punchline: Johnny doubles down with a completely straight-faced, specific defense: he only farms in the suit in good weather
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Three words that are perfectly committed to the bit. Instead of folding, Johnny defends the suit-farming by introducing a weather caveat — as if the real farmers in suits logic is sound, just weather-dependent. The confidence of the delivery implied by the writing is what makes it work. Extremely quotable, excellent economy, only Johnny Rose would say this with a straight face.

jokes.text: Jacob? / Jacob. / Jacob? / What?! / She wants you to pull over. / Oh, I'm sorry, I thought you were talking to the other Jacob.
jokes.setup: The officer addresses 'Jacob' — but both Johnny and Alexis are Jacob
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'I thought you were talking to the other Jacob' — despite there being only one other Jacob (Alexis, who is a woman) — is the payoff of the dual-Jacob naming disaster
jokes.explanation: The Jacob confusion pays off in a satisfying way. The confusion of having two Jacobs is played out through the checkpoint, and 'the other Jacob' is a funny acknowledgment of how absurd the situation is. A solid callback button.

jokes.text: Don't be such a prickly pear.
jokes.setup: David is visibly uncomfortable; Moira calls him in for a chat
jokes.punchline: Moira's unusual term of endearment — 'prickly pear' — is an oddly specific botanical metaphor for David's personality, and only Moira would use it
jokes.explanation: A small but characterful Moira expression. 'Prickly pear' is a perfect description of David AND a very Moira way to say it — botanical, slightly dramatic, vaguely affectionate. The line works because it's simultaneously loving and insulting.

jokes.text: Mostly improved, though I must say I didn't like the matchy matchy skirt and blazer! No, she looked like an ageing stewardess from a Latvian airline!
jokes.setup: Moira is describing having seen Jocelyn looking 'very different' today
jokes.punchline: The critique 'ageing stewardess from a Latvian airline' is a hyper-specific insult that is funnier for its specificity — why Latvian? The precision implies a particular image in Moira's mind
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Ageing stewardess from a Latvian airline' is a masterpiece of specific insult comedy. The Latvian specificity is what elevates it from a generic put-down — it implies Moira has a detailed mental taxonomy of international airline aesthetics, which is exactly right. This is only funny coming from Moira; it's among the most quotable lines in the episode. Near-exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: I told her not to wear the jacket with the skirt, so... I don't know.
jokes.setup: Moira has just criticized Jocelyn's matchy-matchy outfit — the exact jacket-and-skirt combination David told her not to wear
jokes.punchline: David confirming he gave that specific advice — while Moira criticizes the result — is the moment his betrayal becomes visible to the audience, if not fully to Moira yet
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback. David's casual 'I told her not to wear the jacket with the skirt, so...' is simultaneously (a) confirming he was advising Jocelyn, (b) defending his work (he DID say not the jacket), and (c) self-incriminating. The 'so... I don't know' trailing off is perfect. The audience is way ahead of Moira, which creates great dramatic irony.

jokes.text: Now will you be a doll, and fetch Mummy a knife? / A knife? / Oh, I'm sorry, I think you'll find one lodged in the middle of my back.
jokes.setup: David has just confirmed he gave styling advice to Moira's political opponent
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical 'knife in my back' metaphor — asking David to 'fetch' it — turns his betrayal into grand theatrical suffering
jokes.explanation: A classic Moira theatrical mode — she converts genuine hurt into high drama. The 'fetch Mummy a knife' setup is a good misdirect before the metaphor reveals. Her asking David to retrieve the knife from her back is a nice piece of theatrical self-pity.

jokes.text: Okay, well, that's a lot.
jokes.setup: Moira has responded to his betrayal by requesting he fetch a metaphorical knife from her back
jokes.punchline: David's flat 'that's a lot' acknowledges the theatricality without engaging with it — a perfect straight-man response
jokes.explanation: Three words that do everything needed. David is too weary to engage with Moira's theatrics, and his understatement is funnier than any elaborate response would be. A reliable David mode.

jokes.text: And when you pull it out of my back... / Okay, I'm gonna take a shower. / ...you can plunge it into my heart! / Okay, all right. / Plunge it into my heart! (Sighs)
jokes.setup: Moira is escalating the knife metaphor into full theatrical mode
jokes.punchline: David tries to escape to the shower; Moira follows him with 'plunge it into my heart!' — even repeating it as David retreats, sighing
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the knife metaphor to full melodrama — including Moira repeating 'plunge it into my heart!' as David retreats — is a great sustained beat. The sigh at the end perfectly captures Moira's mix of genuine hurt and theatrical excess. A strong mother-son comedy moment.

jokes.text: Well, there it goes. $400.00 worth of milk. / I feel like this is partly my fault. / Partly?!
jokes.setup: The milk has been confiscated at the checkpoint
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'I feel like this is partly my fault' — when she is entirely responsible — is the joke, with Roland's 'Partly?!' nailing the response
jokes.explanation: A nice button to the milk plot. Alexis's minimization of her catastrophic error is perfectly in character, and Roland's single-word 'Partly?!' is the funniest thing Roland says all scene. The structure (understatement → emphatic correction) is reliable but well-executed.

jokes.text: Roland was the one who completely blew it back there! / What?! Did you not see me playing that cop? Oh my God, she didn't know which way was up when I was done with her!
jokes.setup: Alexis deflects blame onto Roland; Roland claims he was brilliant with the officer
jokes.punchline: Roland's delusion that his performance at the checkpoint was masterful — when the audience knows it was disastrous — is classic self-aggrandizing comedy
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious self-congratulation is a well-used character beat. 'She didn't know which way was up when I was done with her' is the right level of absurd overconfidence for a man who panicked at the checkpoint.

jokes.text: The smell of this milk is making me super nauseous.
jokes.setup: The group is standing next to $400 of confiscated raw milk cans
jokes.punchline: In the middle of the post-mortem, Alexis lodges a comfort complaint — the milk smell — which is both self-involved and probably valid
jokes.explanation: A minor character beat — Alexis's physical comfort complaint in an inappropriate moment is a reliable gag. Not particularly inventive but consistent.

jokes.text: Oh come on, guys, let's not end on a down note here, this was a good day, let's end on an up note! / Enough Roland, just get the cans in the truck, please.
jokes.setup: The milk operation has been a complete failure
jokes.punchline: Roland's insistence that 'this was a good day' — and his immediate deflation by Johnny — captures the gap between his optimism and reality
jokes.explanation: A functional tag to the B-plot. Roland's relentless positivity after a disaster is a reliable character beat, and Johnny's curt 'enough Roland' is a good straight-man button.

jokes.text: Oh, okay. I'll grab one. / Pick it up and put it on the truck! / Yes! Oh... okay. / Grab that one, put that on the truck, and then I'll organize the rest so you can grab 'em, and put 'em on the truck.
jokes.setup: Johnny tells Roland to help load the milk cans
jokes.punchline: Roland's 'help' consists of narrating a complex organizational system for Johnny to do the actual work — he'll 'organize' while Johnny carries
jokes.explanation: Roland's self-appointment as logistics coordinator while doing none of the physical work is a decent character beat. The repeat of 'put 'em on the truck' — directed at Johnny to do all the lifting — has a mild escalation.

jokes.text: Someone has to find naked pictures of me. / Better you than a stranger in a storm cellar. / Nothing's coming up. / What search words did you use? / Your name and 'nude.' / Three words I thought I'd only have to type if I was held at gunpoint.
jokes.setup: Moira needs to know if there are compromising photos of her online — presumably for the political campaign
jokes.punchline: A cascade of great lines: 'Better you than a stranger in a storm cellar' (implying where the photos might be found), and Stevie's 'three words I thought I'd only have to type if I was held at gunpoint' (the perfect capper)
jokes.explanation: The episode's best comedic sequence. Multiple jokes compress into a short space. 'Better you than a stranger in a storm cellar' is perfectly Moira — simultaneously self-aware of the photos' existence and theatrical about the alternatives. Stevie's 'three words I thought I'd only have to type if held at gunpoint' is the standout line: it's highly specific, deeply in Stevie's voice (deadpan, beleaguered, imaginative), and captures her relationship with the Roses perfectly. The gunpoint scenario implies that typing '[Moira's name] nude' was something she'd considered a worst-case scenario before meeting this family. Exceptional character integration across all three characters.

tmdb_overview: Johnny gets thrown into the raw milk business. Moira tries to connect with the townspeople. David gives style advice.
insight: Steady character comedy carries 55 jokes at 2.35 per minute, hitting franchise baseline.
